text: The surgeon begins the flap creation on the ipsilateral side, just outside the preperitoneal fat pad, using three eight-millimeter ports on the left side. The dissection follows parallel to the line of the preperitoneal fat pad.
The surgeon uses three 8mm ports on the left side to follow parallel down the line of the preperitoneal fat pad during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection within the preperitoneal fat pad, progressively creating a flap. The video shows the detailed progression of this dissection technique.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection within the preperitoneal fat pad, progressively creating a flap. The video shows the detailed progression of this dissection technique.

The surgeon dissects superiorly and inferiorly around the hernia defect, carefully maintaining the preperitoneal fat layer down and the white posterior sheath up to avoid perforations in the peritoneum or posterior sheath.

The surgeon dissects superiorly and inferiorly around the hernia defect, carefully maintaining the preperitoneal fat pad down and the white posterior sheath up to prevent creating holes in the peritoneum or posterior sheath.
The surgeon extends the dissection flap further inferiorly and attempts to cross over the midline below the hernia defect.
The surgeon extends the dissection flap further inferiorly, attempting to cross over the midline below the hernia defect.

The surgeon approaches the hernia defect, encountering adherent fat to the posterior sheath, necessitating scissor dissection. The epigastric hernia is reached, and dissection occurs above and below the defect, crossing the midline and attempting to access behind it before starting reduction.
The surgeon approaches the hernia defect, encountering adherent fat to the posterior sheath, necessitating scissor dissection. The epigastric hernia is now visible.

The surgeon dissects above and below the hernia defect, crossing the midline and attempting to reach behind the defect. The reduction of the hernia begins, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon reduces the primary hernia, which appears bilobed with a bridge of fascia across it. A large wad of pre-peritoneal fat and phosphor ligaments are visibly stuck into the hernia defect, contributing to the patient's symptoms. The hernia has a small neck with a significant amount of tissue protruding.

The surgeon reduces the primary hernia, which appears bilobed with a bridge of fascia across it. A large wad of pre-peritoneal fat and phosphor ligaments are visible within the hernia defect, contributing to the patient's symptoms. The hernia has a small neck and a significant amount of tissue within the defect.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the contralateral side, closely following the posterior sheath. The dissection within the preperitoneal fat pad is visibly straightforward, with the ease of creating the pocket influenced by the planned mesh size.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the contralateral side, closely following the posterior sheath. The dissection proceeds smoothly within the preperitoneal fat plane, preparing the area for mesh placement.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal flap further to the contralateral side, preparing the surgical field for the placement of a fifteen by fifteen mesh for hernia repair.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal flap significantly farther to the contralateral side compared to the ipsilateral side, preparing the surgical field for the placement of a fifteen by fifteen mesh.

The surgeon positions a large 15x15 mesh centered underneath the hernia defect, ensuring the peritoneal flap extends back to the ipsilateral side beyond the flap creation site. This setup allows the mesh to fit securely within the peritoneal pocket.
The surgeon dissects down towards the umbilicus, uncovering a small hernia defect with incarcerated fat. Umbilical ligaments are visible entering the umbilicus, which the surgeon proceeds to divide.
The surgeon dissects down towards the umbilicus, revealing a small hernia defect with incarcerated fat. Umbilical ligaments are visible entering the umbilicus, which the surgeon proceeds to divide.

The surgeon performs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection with cautery, moving inferiorly to create sufficient space for mesh coverage. The dissection then extends laterally to the contralateral side, ensuring careful manipulation of the tissue.
The surgeon dissects inferiorly to create sufficient space for the placement of a large mesh, ensuring adequate coverage over the hernia defect.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection towards the contralateral side using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques with cautery, carefully navigating the thin tissue to avoid creating holes.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection in the preperitoneal plane, using focused cautery to create a wide pocket slightly larger than fifteen by fifteen centimeters for mesh placement. The transversalis fascia is visible above the dissection area.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection and focused cautery in the preperitoneal plane, creating a wide pocket slightly larger than fifteen by fifteen centimeters for mesh placement, with the transversalis fascia visible above.
The surgeon measures the pocket created for the mesh, ensuring it is large enough to extend across the midline and lay the peritoneal flap over the viscera without difficulty.
The surgeon extends the dissection far to the contralateral side, creating a peritoneal flap that lays across the viscera, ensuring it can be closed past the midline.

The surgeon measures the pocket to confirm its size, noting that the mesh will not be centered due to the narrow creation on the ipsilateral side. The pocket is intended to be fifteen by fifteen centimeters.
The surgeon prepares to place a heavyweight 15x15 mesh, noting that it will not be centered in the pocket due to the narrow creation on the ipsilateral side.

The surgeon selects a heavyweight mesh for the patient due to body habitus and proceeds to sew the hernia defect closed. The inverted pseudosec dermis is imbricated, with bedside confirmation ensuring the needle does not penetrate the skin. The pressure is reduced during closure of the hernia defect.

The surgeon sews the hernia defect closed, imbricating the inverted pseudosec dermis while ensuring the needle does not penetrate the skin. The pressure is reduced during closure, with the final pressure reaching approximately ten.
The surgeon uses a suture to close a small umbilical hernia defect, despite planning to cover it with a large piece of mesh that provides good overlap.
The surgeon uses a suture to close a small umbilical hernia defect, despite planning to cover it with a large piece of mesh that provides good overlap.

The surgeon places a large mesh over the hernia defect, using a 3-0 silk suture to secure it. The suture is threaded through the center of the mesh, then through the hernia defect, and back through the mesh, ensuring the mesh is centered over the defect before tying it in place.
The surgeon places the mesh over the hernia defect, using a 3-0 silk suture to secure it by threading through the mesh and the defect, ensuring the mesh is centered and tied in place.

The surgeon places tacking sutures in the corners of the mesh to prevent rolling, starting with the far right superior corner, followed by the right inferior corner. A suture is then placed on the ipsilateral side to hold the mesh up while preparing to close the peritoneal flap.

The surgeon places tacking sutures in the corners of the mesh to prevent rolling, starting with the far right superior corner, then the right inferior corner, and finally on the ipsilateral side to secure it while suturing the flap closed.

The surgeon adjusts the positioning of the ports, noting the proximity challenges, but successfully manages the flap with sufficient laxity to extend beyond the mesh for complete coverage. Sutures are placed through the fat pad to enhance the strength of the flap closure, preventing tearing.

The surgeon adjusts the positioning of the ports, noting the proximity challenge, but successfully manages the flap with sufficient laxity to cover the mesh completely. Sutures are placed through the fat pad to enhance the strength of the flap closure, preventing tearing.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to close a remaining hole in the mesh, providing a final view of the inverted sac before concluding the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to close a remaining hole, then provides a final view of the inverted hernia sac, indicating the completion of the procedure.
